old_claim.note: Public Key Infrastructure provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Certificate Transparency logs X.509 TLS certificates and validates submitted chains to accepted roots, so an operational public-key certificate infrastructure is constitutive of the protocol.

source_shape.source_locator: Abstract and Section 3.1 define public logging of TLS certificates and require submitted X.509 certificate chains to terminate at roots accepted by the log.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: RFC 6962 defines Certificate Transparency around X.509 certificate-chain submissions, accepted roots, and auditable public log entries.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Without issued certificates, certification chains, and trusted roots there is no protocol object for the scoped log to accept or audit, making PKI constitutive rather than optional.

ontology_before: The graph treated PKI as merely one enabling route to a technology whose protocol is specifically a transparency layer over X.509 certificate issuance.
ontology_after: Certificate Transparency directly requires operational public-key certificate infrastructure and is dated to its first specification in 2013.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-16-certificate-transparency-origin: public_key_infrastructure remains required and the origin remains 2013.
would_reject_if: The node is broadened to generic append-only transparency logs that do not accept or audit public-key certificates.
short_reason: RFC 6962 defines CT as an auditable log of X.509 certificate chains, making PKI a required substrate.
